findings.message: `plan.md` says T001 must land before any file-splitting task, but `impl-plan.json` encodes every task with `depends_on: []`. The machine-readable plan therefore permits the split work to run out of order, which contradicts the stated sequencing requirement.

The three reference entries in `domain-types.json` still have empty `spec_refs` arrays. This scope's checklist requires formal `spec_refs` with SHA-256 hashes, so the type catalogue is not fully grounded as written.

T021 and T022 duplicate T001 instead of adding new work, so S1 now contains three verifier tasks and the plan grows from the briefing's 20 canonical tasks (T001-T020) to 22. That is a semantic mismatch with the stated plan structure and makes the generated `plan.md` task count and S1 checklist misleading as well.

`dry-check.json` is supposed to represent the approved DRY check records for the completed track, but it still contains records with a `violation` verdict. I counted 28 such records, starting here, so the artifact contradicts the completed/approved plan state and would leave unresolved DRY work in the plan artifacts.

`dry-check.json` still records unresolved DRY violations: this record and four later records use a `verdict.violation` object rather than `accepted` or `not-a-violation` (also at lines 3134, 3151, 3181, and 3237). That contradicts the completed/approved plan-artifact state because these pairs remain genuine DRY findings with refactor proposals instead of approved resolutions.

T015 is now marked `done`, but its task text still says the `track/render.rs` split produced `track/render/metadata.rs`. The artifact set recorded in `infrastructure-types.json` and `contract-map.md` instead shows five submodules: `plan`, `registry`, `snapshot`, `sync`, and `validate`. That makes the completed task description out of sync with the actual split scope.

The `#[cfg(test)]` state machine double-counts any line that follows the attribute unless it is a recognized `mod` declaration: `count += 1` runs inside the `saw_cfg_test` branch and then again unconditionally at the end of the loop. That inflates counts for real files that use top-level `#[cfg(test)]` helper items (for example, this repo has `#[cfg(test)] fn` shims in `apps/cli/src/commands/review/codex_local.rs`), so the verifier can emit false max-line errors.

The test-module exclusion only recognizes `mod ` and `pub mod ` immediately after `#[cfg(test)]`. Standard rustfmt-normalized forms already present in this repo, such as `#[cfg(test)] pub(crate) mod test_support {}` and `#[cfg(test)] #[path = "..."] mod tests;`, fall through to the counting path and are treated as production code. That means test-only modules can still push a file over `max_lines`.

The new path filter still counts test-only source files named `tests.rs`. This repo already has large files like `libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/signal_evaluator_v2/tests.rs` and `libs/domain/src/review_v2/tests.rs`, so the verifier will keep emitting max-line errors on test code instead of excluding it.

`count_production_lines()` only excludes inline `#[cfg(test)] mod { ... }` items. Common external test modules declared as `#[cfg(test)] mod tests;` are still counted, so files in this repo that use that pattern (for example `apps/cli/src/commands/review/mod.rs` and `libs/infrastructure/src/review_v2/persistence/mod.rs`) can still hit false module-size violations.

`count_production_lines` only inspects `file.items`, so `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { ... }` nested inside another module is never excluded. Files that use nested test modules will still count test code toward `max_lines`, which can produce false module-size errors.

`count_production_lines` only inspects `file.items`, so it strips top-level `#[cfg(test)] mod ...` blocks but misses the same pattern when it is nested inside another inline module. Those test-only lines will still be counted and can falsely trip the module-size gate.

This skips any file named `tests.rs`, but the task only asked to exclude `_tests.rs` files and directories named `tests/`. A large production module with that name would now bypass the size check entirely.

The cfg(test) exclusion is line-range based, so it drops the entire closing-brace line as soon as a test module is matched. Any production item written on the same physical line as that brace will also be excluded from the count, which undercounts the file and creates a bypass for `max_lines` enforcement.

P1 correctness: [`libs/infrastructure/src/verify/module_size.rs`](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/verify/module_size.rs#L121) only strips inline `#[cfg(test)] mod { ... }` blocks. File-backed test modules declared as `#[cfg(test)] mod tests;` - including the common `#[path = "..._tests.rs"] mod tests;` pattern used elsewhere in this repo - still fall through and are counted as production lines, so files that use that pattern can still trip `max_lines` even though the test code is supposed to be exempt.

`tests.rs` is excluded here, but the brief only asked for `_tests.rs` files and `tests/` directories. That extra basename exemption lets a large production module named `tests.rs` bypass the size gate entirely.

The new file filter still counts plain `tests.rs` modules, because it only skips files ending in `_tests.rs` and directories named `tests/`. This repo has large `#[cfg(test)]`-only modules such as `libs/domain/src/review_v2/tests.rs` and `libs/usecase/src/review_v2/tests.rs`, so the verifier will now emit false size findings on test-only code. Please skip `tests.rs` as well, or otherwise detect file-backed `#[cfg(test)]` modules.

`#[cfg(test)] mod foo;` is only stripped from the declaration span here; the referenced file is still scanned unless its basename happens to match `_tests.rs` or `tests.rs`. That leaves test-only modules with other filenames counted as production code, so the new exclusion is incomplete and can still raise false size violations.

`#[cfg(test)] mod foo;` is returned early here without subtracting the declaration line from the count. Files that use a file-backed test module still keep that `mod ...;` line in the production tally, so an otherwise test-only tail can still push a file over `max_lines` and trigger a false violation.

The new filename filter skips files named exactly `tests.rs`, but the requested production-code exclusions only cover `*_tests.rs`, files under a `tests/` directory, and `#[cfg(test)]` blocks. A production module such as `src/foo/tests.rs` included by an ungated `mod tests;` will now be ignored entirely, letting oversized production code evade the fail-closed module-size check. Remove the `n == "tests.rs"` shortcut unless the file itself is proven test-only via `#![cfg(test)]` or a cfg-gated module declaration.

`collect_rs_files` only suppresses backing files for `#[cfg(test)] mod tests;` when the file is literally `tests.rs`. A file-backed test module such as `#[cfg(test)] mod fixtures;` still has `fixtures.rs` counted as production unless it happens to match `_tests.rs` or live under a `tests/` directory. Since the visitor excludes only the declaration lines, this misses the briefing requirement to handle file-backed `#[cfg(test)] mod` declarations and can make CI fail on test-only helper modules over `max_lines`.

`is_test_only_module_file` only looks for the cfg-gated `mod` declaration in files named as candidates beside the child path (`parent_dir/mod.rs`, `parent_dir/lib.rs`, `parent_dir/main.rs`, or `grandparent/<parent>.rs`). That misses Rust’s valid nested file-backed module case where the parent is inline in an ancestor file, e.g. `src/lib.rs` contains `mod outer { #[cfg(test)] mod fixtures; }` and Rust loads `src/outer/fixtures.rs`. Such a test-only file will be counted as production, and with the new fail-closed max-lines behavior can incorrectly block verification. The file-backed cfg(test) lookup needs to account for inline ancestor declarations or this exclusion path needs a regression test/constraint.

`schema_export` now persists type strings through `signal_evaluator_v2::format::format_type`, but that formatter is a structural-comparison key renderer, not the previous Rust-syntax renderer. It canonicalizes/sorts `dyn`/`impl` bounds and emits extra structural details for variants the old exporter rendered differently, so exporting the same rustdoc JSON can produce different catalogue `TypeRef` strings. This violates the stated no-behavior-change split and can break downstream consumers that expect the schema exporter’s prior TypeRef surface. Keep the old schema-export rendering semantics, or factor out a shared renderer that is explicitly Rust TypeRef syntax preserving.

The new `CfgTestModCollector` accesses `a.pound_token.span` and, later, `s.span`, but `syn` punctuation tokens such as `Pound` and `Semi` expose `spans: [Span; 1]` rather than a `span` field. This makes `libs/infrastructure` fail to compile as soon as `verify::module_size` is type-checked. Use `a.pound_token.spans[0]` / `s.spans[0]` or the `Spanned` trait instead.

`format_type` now delegates to `format_short_type_with_policy`, but that helper does not preserve `Type::FunctionPointer`, `Type::Pat`, or `Type::QualifiedPath`. Those variants now fall through to `_`, so schema export will start emitting lossy TypeRef strings for function-pointer fields and associated-type projections instead of the previous representation.

`has_cfg_test_attr` treats any nested `test` token inside a `cfg` expression as test-only. That means `#[cfg(not(test))]` and mixed gates like `#[cfg(any(test, feature = "test-helpers"))]` both return true. The new module-size verifier then uses this helper to return 0 lines for file-level attrs and to exclude whole modules/files, so production-only or feature-enabled production code can bypass the enforced 700-line limit. Match only an exact test-only cfg before excluding code from production counts.

`collect_rs_files` now skips every directory named `tests` before checking whether the files inside are actually test-only. That is broader than the stated exemption for `tests/` integration tests and `#[cfg(test)]` modules: a production module such as `src/tests/mod.rs` included with an ungated `mod tests;` would be omitted from the production line count, allowing oversized production code to bypass the 700-line enforcement. Restrict this skip to actual integration-test roots or require parent/file-level `#[cfg(test)]` for nested `tests` modules.
